text: when Turner was himself in a liminal state. He had already resigned from Manchester and also sold his house, but he was still waiting for his US visa which was continuously delayed because of his refusal of armed military service during WWII. The Turners were staying at Hastings on the English Channel, living in ‘a state of suspense’ (E. Turner 1985: 7). Turner literally lived at a spatio-temporal threshold when he encountered van Gennep. In contrast to Lévi-Strauss and his British followers, Turner experientially recognized the importance of van Gennep’s insight. The reading inspired him, on the spot, sitting at a small desk in the library at Hastings, to write the essay Betwixt and Between: The Liminal Period in Rites of Passage, the famous chapter in his 1967 publication, The Forest of Symbols. Turner presented the paper once in America, in March 1964, when he had finally taken up his position at Cornell. This would be the first of his explorations into liminality. The reading of van Gennep’s book became a touchstone for everything Turner did, said and thought after 1963. From what can be deduced from Turner’s own references, he did not delve deeper into van Gennep’s larger project. Rites of Passage is practically the only one of van Gennep’s works that Turner ever quoted. The book evidently offered Turner what he was in search of. Van Gennep’s analytical schema further helped Turner to redirect his work beyond the functionalist paradigm. In his analysis of Ndembu ritual, Turner (1967, 1969, 1974) showed how ritual passages served as moments of creativity that freshened up the societal make-up, and argued, against Durkheim (and therefore also against Radcliffe-Brown), that rituals were much more than mere reflections or elaborations of ‘social order’. Van Gennep’s framework and the concept of liminality complemented the term already introduced by Turner: ‘social drama’. Liminality and Ritual Passages among the Ndembu: The Development of Turner’s Approach Turner realized not only that he could adopt van Gennep’s analytical framework; like so many ethnographers who engaged with van Gennep, he recognized that van Gennep’s scheme was inherent in the ritual structure itself, and this was so even with respect to Ndembu emic terms that corresponded in detail to van Gennep’s etic ones. All the Ndembu rituals are characterized by the three-phased processual form of rites of passage (Turner 1967: 13–14): (1) separation (Ilembi or Kulemba), the treatment and dance to make the subjects sacred; (2) margin, a Liminality and the Modern 80 period of seclusion involving partial or complete separation of the subjects from everyday existence; and (3) re-aggregation (Ku-tumbuka), a further treatment and dance celebrating the end of the seclusion period. Van Gennep’s framework brought order into Turner’s data, but it also inspired him to dig deeper. Turner would increasingly insist on the ritual enactment of symbolic orders. He very much followed van Gennep in starting out by stressing the real spatial settings in which ritual transformation took place. This included a focus on the integrative aspects of ritual, but equally on its differentiating ones, such as the distinctions of gender and age groups. The religious component in ritual was now essential for Turner, and he would refuse to treat it like an epiphenomenon. How directly this relates to his own religious conversion is difficult to assess, but there can be little doubt that it had a role to play; or, perhaps rather, it all happened as part of one singular process that involved Turner’s life-work. In his work on the Chihamba ritual, for instance, Turner refused to explain away the religious aspect in ritual: ‘One has to consider religious phenomena in terms of religious ideas and doctrines’ (Turner 1975: 195), almost turning on its head Durkheim’s dictum that social phenomena need social explanation. For Turner this also implied a necessary attention paid to actual religious experience. For Turner ritual is religious, and religion involves both social experiences in concrete ritualistic activity and a systematic corpus of beliefs ‘which have for their object invisible and intangible beings or powers which a human group recognizes as superior, on which it depends’ (V. Turner and E. Turner 1982: 201). In a deeply reflexive vein, Turner (1975: 31–2) went further: ‘After many years as an agnostic and monistic materialist I learned from the Ndembu that ritual and its symbolism are not merely epiphenomena or disguises of deeper social and psychological processes, but have ontological value’. The Later Victor Turner In Turner’s later works (1982, 1985, 1988) a series of further developments took place. It is impossible to talk about any sudden shifts or reorientations. Rather, it seems evident that Turner slowly but surely became more and more interested in the universalistic elements of his approach. He also opened up new empirical fields of interest, including religious pilgrimage and carnivals in ‘complex societies’. His focus remained on ‘social dramas’ and liminal experience, but during the 1970s his fields of reference extended to include literature, film, images and various forms of spectacle. Perhaps most famously, in New York Turner came into contact with theatre and performance groups; this interaction led to an entire Turner-inspired school of performance studies, still today represented by one of Turner’s students there, Richard Schechner. Emblematically, one of Turner’s last books, published after his death, was entitled, From Ndembu to Broadway. The title in a way sums up the travels of the concept of liminality, but it also sums up Turner’s own trajectory. The Turners also went to Brazil, which led to an instant Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 81 analysis of carnival and the Brazilian Umbanda ritual, which, as Turner noted, shared many parallels with Ndembu ritual, and some of them certainly as direct borrowings from African ritual. Finally, Turner took an interest in pilgrimage, again based on a participatory experience with his wife, Edith.
